title: How to Build Your Own Table When There's No Seat for You in Leadership
guestBio: Neetu Sadhwani is a visionary entrepreneur and leader who exemplifies building something meaningful from the ground up. Known for her courage in leaving comfort behind to chase purpose, she's a powerful voice on creating your own opportunities rather than waiting for permission.
excerpt: In the premiere episode of The Manage Her, Aimee sits down with powerhouse Neetu Sadhwani to discuss the bold decision to leave comfort behind and build your own opportunities. This conversation dives deep into burnout, courage, and why waiting for a seat at the table is no longer an option.
metaDescription: Neetu Sadhwani shares how she built her own table in leadership, overcame burnout, and found the courage to chase purpose over comfort on The Manage Her podcast.
keyTakeaways: Building your own table is more powerful than waiting for someone else to offer you a seat.
Leaving comfort behind for purpose requires courage, but it's essential for authentic leadership.
Burnout is often a signal that you're not aligned with your true purpose and values.
Grace, grit, and vision are the three pillars needed to build something meaningful from scratch.
Believing in yourself even when it's hard is a non-negotiable skill for women leaders.
Real balance isn't about perfect equilibrium but about making intentional choices aligned with your priorities.
Women are more capable than they think, but permission-giving starts from within.

pullQuotes.text: Why waiting for a seat at the table just isn't it anymore — she built her own

content:
Welcome to the very first episode of The Manage Her podcast, where we're setting the tone for everything this show represents: honest conversations, powerful women, and the kind of permission-giving energy that reminds you just how capable you really are.

I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, and I couldn't be more excited to kick things off with the incredible Neetu Sadhwani. If you've ever felt like you're waiting for someone else to make room for you at the leadership table, this conversation is going to shift something fundamental in how you think about building your career and your life.

The Courage to Leave Comfort Behind

One of the most powerful themes that emerged from my conversation with Neetu is this idea of having the courage to leave comfort behind in pursuit of something bigger. We live in a world that constantly tells us to play it safe, to take the predictable path, to wait for permission. But what Neetu discovered, and what she shares so beautifully in this episode, is that real fulfillment comes when you're willing to step into the unknown.

The truth is, comfort can become a cage. You might have a good job, a steady paycheck, and the approval of everyone around you, but if you're not aligned with your purpose, you're slowly dying inside. Neetu talks about that moment when she realized she had to make a choice: stay safe or step into her power. The decision wasn't easy, but it was necessary.

This resonates so deeply with me because I see it everywhere in my work with women leaders. You have this incredible vision, this knowing that you're meant for something more, but the gap between where you are and where you want to be feels insurmountable. What Neetu teaches us is that the bridge across that gap is built one courageous decision at a time.

Building Your Own Table

Here's where the conversation gets really juicy. We've all heard the phrase about getting a seat at the table, right? Well, Neetu flips that entire narrative on its head. Instead of waiting for someone else to make room for her, she decided to build her own table. And not just any table, but one that reflects her values, her vision, and her definition of success.

This mindset shift is everything. When you're waiting for a seat at someone else's table, you're operating from scarcity. You're hoping someone will see your value and invite you in. But when you build your own table, you're operating from abundance. You're creating the rules, setting the standards, and inviting others to join you in something meaningful.

The beauty of building your own table is that you get to decide what it looks like. You don't have to conform to outdated leadership models that were never designed with women in mind. You can create something that honors your strengths, accommodates your life, and makes space for the kind of leader you actually want to be.

The Real Talk on Burnout

We couldn't have this conversation without diving deep into burnout, because let's be honest, it's an epidemic among ambitious women. Neetu shares her own experience with burnout and the insights that came from it. What she discovered is that burnout isn't just about being too busy or working too hard. It's often a signal that you're not aligned with your purpose.

When you're pouring your energy into something that doesn't light you up, when you're trying to succeed in systems that weren't built for you, when you're constantly pushing against your natural rhythms and instincts, your body and mind will eventually say no. Burnout becomes the wake-up call that forces you to examine whether you're living your life or someone else's version of what your life should be.

The recovery from burnout isn't just about rest and self-care, though those things matter. It's about realigning with what actually matters to you. It's about having the courage to make different choices, even when those choices disappoint other people or challenge their expectations of you.

Grace, Grit, and Vision

Throughout our conversation, Neetu embodies what I call the trifecta of successful leadership: grace, grit, and vision. These aren't just nice qualities to have; they're essential tools for building something meaningful from the ground up.

Grace is about how you show up in the world. It's about maintaining your integrity and treating people well, even when things get challenging. It's about leading with empathy and understanding that everyone is fighting their own battles.

Grit is about your willingness to keep going when things get hard. And they will get hard. Building anything worthwhile requires perseverance, resilience, and the ability to pivot when your original plan isn't working.

Vision is about seeing possibilities that others can't see yet. It's about holding onto a clear picture of what you're creating, even when everyone around you thinks you're crazy. It's about trusting your instincts and betting on yourself when no one else will.

Believing in Yourself When It's Hard

Perhaps the most important theme from this conversation is the idea of believing in yourself even when it's hard. Especially when it's hard. Because here's what I know to be true: if you don't believe in yourself, no one else will. And believing in yourself isn't a one-time decision. It's a daily practice, especially when you're building something new.

There will be days when you question everything. When the criticism feels too loud, when the progress feels too slow, when the mountain you're climbing seems impossibly tall. In those moments, you have to come back to what you know to be true about yourself and your vision.

This is where community becomes so important. Surrounding yourself with other women who understand the journey, who can reflect back your strength when you can't see it yourself, who can remind you of how far you've already come. That's part of what The Manage Her is about, creating that community of women who are committed to lifting each other up.

As we wrap up this inaugural episode, I want you to remember that you are more capable than you think. The dreams you have, the vision that keeps you up at night, the feeling that you're meant for something more? Trust that. Honor that. And most importantly, take action on that.

This is just the beginning of our journey together on The Manage Her podcast. Every episode, we're going to dive into these real, honest conversations about what it means to be a woman building something meaningful in the world. And I can't wait to continue this conversation with you.

quiz.title: Are You Ready to Build Your Own Table?
quiz.description: Discover whether you're someone who waits for permission or creates your own opportunities. Take this quiz to uncover your unique leadership approach and how you can step into your power.

questions.question: When you imagine your ideal work situation five years from now, what feels most exciting to you?

options.text: Leading something I created from scratch, even if it means starting over
Having a senior role where I can implement my strategic vision
Being recognized as an expert in my field with increasing responsibility
Working with amazing people on meaningful projects that make a difference

questions.question: You're in a meeting where you have a brilliant idea, but it challenges the current approach. What's your typical response?

options.text: I speak up immediately—good ideas shouldn't wait for the 'right' moment
I think it through first, then schedule time to present it properly with supporting data
I share it if asked directly, but I'm careful about how I frame it
I find a way to suggest it that builds on what others have already said

questions.question: When you think about taking a big career risk, what's usually your biggest concern?

options.text: That I might not execute my vision as well as I imagine it
That I haven't considered all the potential obstacles and backup plans
That I might be giving up security and progress I've already made
That it might negatively impact the people who depend on me

questions.question: How do you typically respond when you're feeling burned out or misaligned at work?

options.text: I start planning my exit strategy and researching new possibilities
I analyze what's not working and develop a plan to address it systematically
I focus on the positive aspects and try to find ways to grow within my current situation
I talk it through with trusted friends and family to get their perspective

questions.question: What does 'believing in yourself' look like for you on a practical level?

options.text: Trusting my instincts even when others think I'm being too bold
Having confidence in my ability to solve problems I haven't encountered yet
Knowing that my consistent effort and dedication will pay off over time
Remembering that my perspective and contributions matter, even when I doubt myself

questions.question: When you hear the phrase 'building your own table,' what's your honest first reaction?

options.text: Yes! I've been waiting for permission to do exactly that
It sounds appealing, but I want to understand exactly what that would look like first
It's intriguing, but I'm not sure I'm ready to leave behind what I've already built
I love the idea, but I wonder if there's a way to do it that brings others along too

results.title: The Table Builder
results.description: You're naturally drawn to creating new opportunities rather than waiting for someone else to invite you in. You have strong instincts about when it's time to leave comfort behind and the courage to act on them. Your challenge is trusting that your vision is worth the risk. This episode will validate your table-building instincts and give you the framework of grace, grit, and vision you need to build something truly meaningful.

results.title: The Thoughtful Strategist
results.description: You believe in thorough preparation and careful planning before making big moves. You're incredibly capable but sometimes get stuck in analysis mode when what you really need is to trust yourself and leap. You have all the skills to build your own table—you just need permission to believe in your readiness. This episode will help you recognize that waiting for the 'perfect' moment might be holding you back from your true purpose.

results.title: The Steady Climber
results.description: You're committed to growth and achievement, but you prefer working within existing structures rather than creating entirely new ones. You're incredibly reliable and others trust you to deliver, but you might be playing it too safe. Your superpower is your consistency, but your growth edge is learning when comfort becomes a cage. This episode will inspire you to consider that building your own table might be the next level of leadership you've been preparing for all along.

results.title: The Harmony Seeker
results.description: You're deeply thoughtful about how your choices affect others and you value collaboration and balance above all else. You might struggle with the idea of 'building your own table' because it feels selfish or disruptive. But your natural ability to consider multiple perspectives is actually a leadership superpower. This episode will help you understand that building your own table can be an act of service—creating space for others who also need a seat.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager: Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, hitting Amazon May 1st, 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in eight figures, and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and film production. I'm a mom of six, and this is The Manager podcast where we're digging into the unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

I'm stoked to introduce Neetu Sadhwani, president of Sunrise Group, Inc., a brilliant IT services business owner and my dear friend. We met through Women's Business Enterprise National Council. We bonded over our love for breaking barriers. Neetu is running a tech empire, raising two daughters and cooking mind-blowing Indian food from scratch with a wit that cracks me up. 

Neetu, with chapter one in hand, what's the most mundane of your invisible labor you handle keeping it all going at home and in the business?

**Neetu:** Oh my gosh, the most mundane. It's like you work with muscle memory. You have the daily routine in your head. Like, okay, the first thing I'm gonna do is I'm going to have a team call. My team's overseas, right? 6:30, we start a team call. I don't even have to think about it. It just happens naturally.

For 30 minutes, we go over to-do lists. What are the requirements? Who's going to do what? Who's leading the team? Who's working first and who's going second? And then we just take turns. And then as I'm getting done with the call, the mom wakes up. The mom hat. 

So this hat off, this hat on. So I'm having my tea, trying to pack up something for lunches. The daughter comes downstairs as always. Like she's running late. Always. And I'm like, "Dude..." But she goes, "No, I have to be at school in 10 minutes." I'm like, "It's a 20 minute drive." But I don't know how. Then I become a race car driver.

**Aimee:** Me too.

**Neetu:** And I get there in 9 minutes. I'm like it just happens like I don't know how but it happens. So a lot of mundane things but the invisible comes in between those like you don't realize it. That I become a race car driver like I'm driving and in that 9 minutes drive, mind you, I have my team on the call with me. We are going to schedule an interview. Can you interview somebody in 15 minutes? And I'm like will I be at home in 15 minutes or will I be at my desk in 15 minutes? And I just say yes. Yeah, sure. Let's go. Let it roll.

**Aimee:** It's like you have like 10 hands. You're doing multitasking. Multitasking always. All the time. And thank God for cell phones because you can do a Zoom in your car.

**Neetu:** And thank God for Bluetooth in the car also. Without technology, I don't think I'll be able to.

**Aimee:** Me too. It's just so reliant, so dependent on it. The more technology I have, the more I seem to be able to do. Which is kind of cool. And that's what I need also. Being in IT, technology becomes your friend. And the more you get used to of it, the more you become comfortable with it. I think it's powerful.

**Neetu:** Oh, totally. And I've gotten to see being on this digital transformation ride with these big companies and seeing how it really has transformed their organizations for sure. Automating mundane tasks and using AI to take data from a million different places and put it in one place.

We did something similar for our client and then nobody understood what was going on at that time and COVID hit and I'm like the timings couldn't have been perfect. Because you want to move everything from on-premise to cloud everything at that time. Nobody knew what's coming. March 28th and everybody's like nobody's at office and the on-premise server cannot handle anything. 

And I'm like, okay, let's try it. Because I did something similar but on a very smaller scale. But we did something super big and everybody was happy and that was like my moment and I'm like, okay, we are still good. We thought everything was done and over with when COVID hit.

**Aimee:** And it was really the beginning, not the end. It was just the beginning, but a lot of good came out of it. Like the digital transformation. So funny. It was like we're doing this work and the word digital transformation didn't even exist.

**Neetu:** Oh my gosh. Yes. And it was like finally I was working with Shell and they have a digital transformation lab and I was like that's the word. It's digital transformation. Thank you for giving me the word for what we do here.

**Aimee:** You were at WE Bank National last year. 

**Neetu:** Yes. So I was at a booth and they gave me a topic. They said you need to talk about digital transformation and AI and I'm like sure I can do it. And a lot of people who were walking by first nobody was interested. Because we talk these big words and then not everybody's so comfortable with our tech jargon. A lot of people are afraid of it.

So, we made it super simple. We talked about it and I brought in AI and this lady goes, "I'm afraid of AI." And I'm like, "Please don't." Because we all were thinking like when it started like, "Oh my gosh, there won't be any developer jobs because you can have ChatGPT, you can have these tools type your stuff for you." And yeah, we don't need any programmers, but they don't realize that talking to AI, it's a whole new job.

**Aimee:** It's like having paints and a paintbrush and expecting a Monet. You still need an artist. You still need leadership. I think what AI has done is it has made our jobs easier. More productive and faster. Nobody's going to lose their job. I think people need to know that.

**Neetu:** I do too. It's just it'll be nice for people because they're not going to have to do the mundane tasks that they were doing. We had this woman at this large, world's second largest food and beverage company and very first meeting we had at their headquarters. We're on a Zoom, checking our papers, it's COVID, the place is empty, there's like two people from the company sitting in the room and everyone else is on a Zoom with us. 

And she is saying "I'm still pushing orders by hand. I'm inputting orders by hand." And my VP was like "I got a software for that, we can fix that, we can automate that." It was a couple of years later, we ended up back at this company on a panel as successful vendors on this panel as panelists and we got to answer this question about AI and talk about this woman who had a master's degree who was doing this mundane labor of hand putting orders into the system who now can automate her task and do higher level tasks.

**Aimee:** So, it saves you time, but it also saves like that mind share. You know, because it's out of mind. It's done. And taken away from her real gift to the company which is like this upper level intelligence that she has.

**Neetu:** And then I feel like it's very cost-saving also. Having that being automated. Because when you use the word mundane, there's so many examples that come in your head. But imagine if these are automated. Imagine if you just prompt something and it happens. All you have to do is put new name. 

Let's say you're on-boarding an employee rather than doing everything. That's what we do. We used to write like the entire MSA or entire onboarding documents. Now everything is set. All you have to do is put your name in the software and it just auto-generates things.

**Aimee:** That's beautiful. Less room for mistakes. And you're saving time, you're saving money, things are moving faster, clients are happy. Everyone's happy. And your employees are happy because why have them have to go through this whole document, make all these changes by hand, old school.

**Neetu:** I think having that mundane task being automated, I think it should be done everywhere. If I could do it in the kitchen, I would do it.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. Right. For me, it's like, please automate my laundry. Someone come along. And I just don't like folding it. I don't mind putting it in the machine. I wish that the machine was a washer and a dryer. I don't understand the two. Like, why can't it wash and dry? Why do I have to manually move it? And then, can it please fold itself? I would even fold it. I would even iron it. What I hate is putting it back.

**Neetu:** For me, it's the folding and but you're right. Putting it away is another task because nobody does it. Like I'll do mine and then everybody else, but when it comes to kids, no, they like living off of the baskets.

**Aimee:** Oh yeah. I think my son's living off the clothes on the floor actually. And they know it. I'm very particular about laundry. How it's being done. Dishwasher and laundry. They are like my pet peeves. I'm like, you have to do it properly.

**Neetu:** Well, it's nice when it's done right. It really is so nice. I'm trying to implement the dishwasher more at my house. It's part of my new automation strategy. For some strange reason, I'm still handwashing my dishes and I have a dishwasher. So, that's when I was writing my book, one of my SOPs that I'm going to change in my house is for us to use the dishwasher because seriously, Aimee, automate things. Digital transformation.

**Aimee:** I know it's like digital transformation from like the 1970s. Here we go.

**Neetu:** Now, at my home, I have to put the dishes in the dishwasher. Like, nobody's allowed to touch it. And if they put it, I will make sure that I am checking it before I turn on the start button. And I'm like, no, it has to be put in a certain way. Otherwise, I'm just going to redo it.

**Aimee:** That's so us though. Like that's so us, managing but sometimes micromanaging our homes too, right? Because I have six children, I'm really learning not to micromanage. I'm trying to learn to manage my children like they're employees. Because I actually have this crazy idea that I'm going to be writing employee handbooks for each one of the kids with SOPs.

**Neetu:** Oh my goodness.

**Aimee:** Because I want them to have to sign it. I want to sign off on it and like actually know that I've communicated as leadership in the house that I've fully communicated my expectations to them in writing like I would an employee. And so this is my crazy new idea and I actually talked about it in my book and it's one of the things I'm like could we run our businesses more like we run our homes which we run our homes so effortlessly. 

And bringing that effortless leadership into business I think is a beautiful thing that women can do. But also bringing that structured leadership into our homes having rules and having certain procedures to follow.

**Neetu:** Definitely. I think if we're using our real people management skills, our HR skills in our homes and really effectively communicating with our children as if they're our employees because it's like we don't as leadership want to get that resentment towards them. Like why can't you just put your clothes in the laundry basket? Like I feel resentful when I walk by your room and your clothes are all over the floor. 

But am I effectively communicating that resentment? I don't want to resent that because it gets ugly, right, with our relationships with people. So, I'm wondering if there might not be this beautiful way that women can actually use the skills from work in our homes and the skills from home in our work.

**Aimee:** I think we can and we do already, don't we? We really do. Subconsciously because women are born multitaskers. We are not just like a mom or a business owner or a daughter or a wife, right? We are doing so many things at one time that inherently we are meant or we are born to multitask. 

So without knowing sometimes I feel like I bring that business aptitude that I have in my household or vice versa. And it happens naturally and it has worked beautifully also like I think it's amazing how these two can come in the picture and they streamline themselves. It just works wonders.

**Neetu:** And that's really the reason why I wrote this book is I really feel like we as women have always like for millennia we have been leaders. We have always been in management. We have always been in operations. It was just in the domestic realm and because we weren't getting paid for it we didn't get the recognition for it. But now that we're actually in leadership positions we can actually look at what we're doing in our home and go hey wait. I see that as an HR move right there. I see that as AP. I see that as an accounting position, right? Skilled labor.

**Aimee:** And that's when in your first chapter when you talk about your invisible skills. It's that's what it is. And like you said, we have done these multitasking leadership roles forever, right? It's just a matter of us bringing it outside of our house now. And guess what? We don't even need any training because we've been doing it forever. Well, we've been watching our mothers and our grandmothers do it, too. And our aunties and everybody.

I was reading somewhere. It's like when men go to bed, they just sleep, right? Like they go to bed, they fall asleep and I had a conversation with my partner. So I'm like, "What happens when you put your head on the pillow?" And he goes, "I sleep." And I'm like, "Do you think about tomorrow?" And he goes a little bit, but to me it doesn't end there. 

Like as soon as I put my head on the pillow I'm rewinding the whole day like what happened, what could have been done differently, whether it's work or whether it's kids or whether it's friends also because we have so many different titles around us and then when I'm putting my head on the pillow I'm like okay what do I need to do tomorrow? Like I had this email after the work was ended so I need to share this with my team.

**Neetu:** Totally. I literally have a diary.

**Aimee:** I do too. If I don't write it down, because I'll lay down and then I'll remember all the things I need to do tomorrow and I won't be able to sleep the whole night if I don't write it down. Just write your to-do list and then get a good night's sleep.

**Neetu:** And in your head, that's what's happening. Like, are you working with your team? Do I need to do this? Was I supposed to do this? Like Rosie has this activity tomorrow, so I need to go to school at this time. I need to see her get this award. And don't forget to buy milk.

**Aimee:** Don't forget to buy milk. I have many times gone to Costco or any grocery stores like I have a long list as soon as I get to the store and I'm like I forgot my list.

**Neetu:** It's the worst. It is. It's actually the worst. I put it in my notes in my phone now because I always have my phone with me and I used to love my paper lists but now I just do it in my notes in my phone and then I always have it with me because I do the same thing. You get there and you don't have it and then I can't remember. I'll get home with like 50% of the things I need.

**Aimee:** I would get home with 150% and the one thing that was needed that won't be in that 150%.

**Neetu:** Exactly. I know. It's so true. And we're as women, we're just constantly doing so many things, but we work in IT and it is kind of still a man's world. And could you give me an example? Like we talk about the patriarchal wound, the wound of the patriarchy in chapter one and like being dismissed or those kinds of experiences that we've had with men in business. What have you had as experiences like that?

I'll share two examples with you. First when I was not an entrepreneur when I was working in IT and I was not in a leadership role but working with a project manager. And the whole like the project manager is telling you what to do. I mean it's a PM's job to come up with the plan but the team does the work. 

So I was a business analyst. My job was to make sure all the three teams have the requirements, have the documents that everybody needs and I just write everything and I hand it over to the project manager. This particular project before my team came along with the project manager and the other team members it was never successful. So when my team came in we all worked together and everybody should get the credit not just me. So everybody got credit except for me.

**Aimee:** Oh.

**Neetu:** And that PM when he presented the project and it was done on time on budget and everything was done and again like it wasn't just me who did everything. But everybody got credit. I was the only female by the way in that team of seven or eight I want to say. So it happens. We get overlooked. And I was almost like a business analyst almost like a junior PM. 

And when the project presentation was done, my name was not there. I thought maybe it was overlooked. Whatever, it happens, right? And when the project manager at the end of the presentation, the client came and he goes, "We are just so in awe like how did you guys do it? How did you manage it?" And then he was going through everybody and when it came to my turn, the client was like, "Oh, you have an amazing team." But the project manager, he goes, "I have an amazing team, but without her, we won't be able to do it because we were all giving her beads, she turned it into jewelry, right?" 

So that's the kind of like people do overlook and I don't know, first I thought maybe because I'm Indian, maybe because I'm...

**Aimee:** You're a woman and you're a woman of color. It's a double. It really is the double. It is.

**Neetu:** So, but I almost was in tears. Like you can't show your tears at workplace. Which I think we should be able to. There's nothing wrong with that. And that's like this is like 15, 16, 17 years back. So that time it's like me being new in IT. And it's a man's world. And it still is. And at that time it was actually worse. Because it just happens to be like that. 

And I'll give you another example of me walking into the room and they're still thinking like a CEO is going to walk in and in their head it's like the CEO is maybe... So you're sitting around waiting for someone and they're waiting for someone and you're like who are we waiting for? And they're like we're waiting for one more person. And I'm like okay. And I'm like who is it? And he goes your CEO. And I'm like excuse me like I'm here. I am the CEO. And they were all shocked, right? 

And because they don't, that's not the first thing that comes to their mind, right? Women being president and CEO of IT.

**Aimee:** No, definitely not. Like if it was a restaurant and if it was something else. Restaurants. If it's a stationary store. It's a woman's job. Like no. Sorry. No. IT CEO. No.

**Neetu:** Definitely. I think we're still getting our representation here. We're still becoming, we are the representatives of women in IT at high levels and leadership positions and we're showing these young women like hey no we're here. Now we're here and then before I wouldn't introduce myself as a president and CEO because I feel like am I bragging about it right but it's just your title and they need to know.

So it wasn't entirely their fault. Like maybe when I walked in, I should have said like, "Hey, I'm Neetu. I'm president of Sunrise." They didn't know, right? But at the same time, like for them to just have that mindset.

**Aimee:** It's an old paradigm. It was an old paradigm where every CEO is an old white man. Like guess what? Not always. Not anymore, boys. Not anymore. We can play the same game, too.

So, I'm sure there's been a moment where your brilliance has been overlooked. How did you flip it? How were you able to flip a moment?

**Neetu:** I think it's the matter of getting comfortable first of all. Because unless you're not comfortable with your work, and you haven't accepted it, you won't be able to convince others. There shouldn't be any convincing needed. 

Similar things happened like walking into a room where people won't recognize you and when you're talking people won't let you talk and if I bring in my people from my team they're all male like you have business development and all that when they are talking it just like everybody's listening. 

So I'll give you another example at WE Bank Nationals table. We were all sitting in open rounds and I go and this is a huge automotive company and this was in Tennessee. So, we go there and for some reason I was the only one who was female. And we're all talking and then for some reason instead of going in circle he picked me as a third person to go and I gave him my spiel like this is what we do, why value added whatever and I didn't bring in my certifications because I've learned that certifications are not your value added. It's just something that I do. This is going to help you. But I'm not going to tell you. That's the only thing that's value added about me. 

So, we go and then I'm talking and he goes, "What do you know about this?" And I'm like, "A lot, but we don't have enough time. We only have 20 minutes and I want to make sure I want to be respectful for everybody's time because everybody needs to get the turn." But I'm like, he didn't ask this from the other men, right? 

And then comes again next question. He goes, "How are you different than others?" And I'm like, "You know what?" We are all doing staffing. We are all doing services. We are just here to please you as a client. 

So if you give me a job, I will put in 100%. I will personally get involved because you're a new client. You're working with a small business. You're getting an incredible customer service.
